ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic omelette food product class; no specific Filipino onion omelette entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific onion omelette entry. A00LC (Hen's egg, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for onion egg omelette. No code assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 120 g, equivalent to a two-egg omelette with diced onion cooked in a small amount of oil.

nutrient_source.title: Eggs, whole, cooked, scrambled / omelette with onion — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01132 adapted with onion component)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked whole eggs (NDB 01132, scrambled/omelette) combined with USDA data for raw onion (NDB 11282) at approximately 15 g onion per 100 g dish. Weighted composite calculated to reflect a standard onion egg omelette. No direct HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry for this specific dish was found.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; egg-based dishes and omelette analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for onion egg omelette. GI estimated at 32 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for egg-based dishes (whole egg omelette, GI ~32, low category). Onion contributes minimal carbohydrate and does not materially alter GI. Glycemic load per 120 g serving calculated as (2.8 g carbohydrate × 120 / 100) × 32 / 100 = 1.1 g GL.

hypertension.guidance: At approximately 230 mg sodium per 100 g, a 120 g serving contributes about 276 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should monitor total daily sodium intake and consider preparing this dish with reduced added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 32 and only 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this omelette has a negligible glycemic load and is generally suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when prepared with minimal added oil and salt.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol and saturated fat from whole eggs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 120 g serving provides approximately 353 mg dietary cholesterol and 3.6 g saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit egg yolk consumption in line with current clinical guidelines and discuss frequency with their healthcare provider.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion Egg Omelette (Filipino: Itlog na may Sibuyas) is a simple, everyday Filipino egg dish consisting of beaten whole eggs pan-fried with diced or sliced onions in a small amount of cooking oil. The eggs are whisked until fluffy, combined with finely chopped onion, seasoned with salt, and cooked in a lightly oiled pan over medium heat until set and lightly golden on both sides. The result is a soft, tender omelette with a mildly sweet savory flavour from the caramelised onion. It is a staple breakfast and all-day meal in Filipino households and is commonly served alongside steamed white rice and a dipping sauce of soy sauce with calamansi. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of high-quality complete protein (approximately 10 g per 100 g), fat-soluble vitamins A, D, and E from the egg yolk, and B-vitamins including riboflavin and B12. The carbohydrate content is very low (approximately 2.8 g per 100 g), giving it a low glycemic index of approximately 32 and a negligible glycemic load per serving. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Sodium content is moderate and can be controlled by reducing added salt during preparation. The cholesterol content from egg yolks is clinically relevant for individuals with cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, this dish appears on Filipino café menus and is a familiar comfort food for the Filipino diaspora community.
common_user_queries: Filipino egg omelette with onion calories
Is onion egg omelette good for diabetics?
How many calories in a Filipino omelette?

confidence_reason: No exact match for onion egg omelette was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked whole eggs and raw onion using a weighted composite approach. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for egg-based omelette dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: cooked whole egg (NDB 01132) at approximately 85 g per 100 g dish and raw onion (NDB 11282) at approximately 15 g per 100 g dish, with minor oil contribution. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for this egg preparation; naturally zero in unhydrogenated cooking oils). No sentinel -1 values are used for core macros; all secondary nutrients with -1 are not applicable here as all have been populated from USDA data. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Filipino onion omelette was located in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. Cholesterol content is clinically significant at approximately 294 mg per 100 g. The food_group 'Dairy and eggs' is preserved from the input record as eggs are the primary ingredient. The food_name has been expanded from 'Onion Egg' to 'Onion Egg Omelette' to provide a more canonical and unambiguous English food name. The cuisine is Filipino; however, because this dish is served in Singapore by a Filipino café, multilingual names for the Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) have been provided in addition to the Filipino romanized name. Ontology codes use closest_parent match types as no exact ontology entries exist for this specific dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Expanded food_name from 'Onion Egg' to 'Onion Egg Omelette' for canonical clarity.
Added full multilingual local_names for Singaporean context (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) plus Filipino romanized name.
Derived nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy using weighted composite of cooked egg and raw onion components.
Estimated GI at 32 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous egg omelette entry with documented basis.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as confirmed zero per USDA source.
Set ontology match types to closest_parent with gap notes; no codes invented.
